patient.instructions:

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 9-year-old boy (3rd grade). You act anxious and speak vaguely, needing gentle prompting. Start with: “I keep getting fevers that come and go, and I feel wiped out.” Volunteer only: fevers every few weeks sometimes with chills; tired/weak most days; look pale and get short of breath on stairs; get lots of colds/sinus infections.

If asked for details, add: fevers last 2–4 days then stop; mouth sores during some episodes; tender neck glands; many ear infections when younger; bruise easily and sometimes nosebleeds that take a while; feel full quickly after eating; cold/tingly hands/feet when very tired; schoolwork/learning and planning take longer.

Do not mention unless directly asked: dizziness standing, craving chewing ice, racing heart with short walks, slow growth, peeing a lot at night, leg cramps/weakness after illnesses, foamy urine, weight loss.

Resist/deny at first: painful genital/anal sores—only admit if asked privately and reassured.

Misconceptions: say fevers feel “like clockwork,” worry about juvenile arthritis or a “cyclic blood problem.”

If clinician shows empathy, you relax, give clearer timelines. If costs come up, echo family worries about “too many tests,” skipping follow-ups, or expensive immune meds since you “look fine” between fevers.

tool_call_sequence.description: Establishes the working diagnosis so treatment is appropriately targeted and can be explained to the patient.
Initiates evidence-based therapy (iron replacement) while ensuring required medication safety checks are completed before prescribing.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as iron deficiency anemia and links it to low iron in plain language.
Explains the treatment plan and explicitly includes an iron supplement as the primary therapy.

Before prescribing, performs a contraindication check (critical) relevant to iron therapy and the patient context (e.g., iron overload disorders, repeated transfusions/known hemochromatosis, inability to tolerate oral iron, suspected ongoing significant bleeding requiring urgent evaluation).

Before prescribing, performs a drug interaction check (critical), including common interaction patterns for oral iron (reduced absorption with antacids/PPIs, calcium, levothyroxine, tetracyclines/fluoroquinolones; advises dose separation).
Before prescribing, asks about medication allergies/intolerances (non-critical but required) and documents/acts on any reported reactions.
Provides dosing/administration guidance for iron (how to take, separation from interacting agents, expectations for response) without unsafe or clearly incorrect dosing.

Counsels on common adverse effects (GI upset, constipation, dark stools) and mitigation strategies; includes warning signs that require prompt care (e.g., melena/hematochezia, syncope, chest pain, severe shortness of breath).

Addresses etiology evaluation at a minimum by asking about bleeding sources and risk factors (heavy menses, GI symptoms, NSAID use, dietary intake) and recommends appropriate follow-up to identify the cause.

Creates a follow-up plan in natural language even if not using a follow-up tool (e.g., timeframe to reassess symptoms and repeat CBC/ferritin), and sets expectations for duration of therapy (continue after normalization to replete stores).
Uses patient-centered communication: checks understanding, invites questions, and avoids blaming language.
